feedback.text: One game I am very fond of is Ubisoft's Tom Clancy's Ghost Recon: Breakpoint. It is a military-themed third person shooting game which, despite its flaws and mixed critical and public opinion, is near and dear to my heart. The game, despite being completely playable from beginning to end as a single-player experience (with that experience being the default, expected one), has an arbitrary always-on internet requirement to connect to Ubisoft servers so they can store your character's progress. We've had the technology to save games on our consoles and computers for decades, so there was never a need for this system to exist. And because of it, even though the game runs everything locally, from physics to enemy behavior to damage calculations, one day Ubisoft will just pull the plug and my purchase and access to a beloved game will become null. I believe in Stop Killing Games because it stops publishers from artificially creating expiration dates for what is essentially a new form of art. I don't think it'd be okay to burn the Mona Lisa because it is over 300 years old and its had its time in the spotlight. I think that same credit should be given for games. People should own the games they own, and have access to them for as long as they live and breathe - And ideally, their kids and grandkids too.
